steps.title: Recognize when a system has infinitely many solutions
steps.content: A system of two linear equations has infinitely many solutions when the equations represent the same line. This occurs when one equation is a scalar multiple of the other — all corresponding coefficients and the constant must share the same ratio.

steps.title: Find the scaling factor using the x-coefficients
steps.content:
If Equation 2 is a scalar multiple $c$ of Equation 1, then the x-coefficients must satisfy:
$$c \cdot \frac{4}{5} = \frac{8}{13}$$
Solving for $c$:
$$c = \frac{8}{13} \cdot \frac{5}{4} = \frac{40}{52} = \frac{10}{13}$$

steps.title: Apply the scaling factor to find g/k
steps.content:
Since Equation 2 is obtained by multiplying Equation 1 by $c = \frac{10}{13}$, the constant term is also scaled by this factor:
$$c \cdot g = k$$
Therefore:
$$\frac{g}{k} = \frac{1}{c} = \frac{13}{10} = 1.3$$
